source_metadata.title_raw: Jim Karpiak - A-19-06 - June 14, 2019 - Palm Desert

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 14, 2019

Jim Karpiak
Executive Director
Coachella Valley Mountains Conservancy
73-710 Fred Waring Drive, Suite 112
Palm Desert, CA 92260

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-19-086

Dear Mr. Karpiak:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other conflict of interest laws. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit you, the Executive Director of the Coachella Valley Mountains Conservancy, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the Palm Springs Cemetery District's application for a grant under the Conservancy's Proposition 1 Grant Program to remove tamarisk trees from real property owned by the District and replace those trees with native trees or shrubs given that your residence is located approximately 150 feet from that real property and approximately 661 feet from the nearest trees that the District would remove pursuant to the grant?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the District's grant application because those decisions' effect on your real property interest in your residence would be nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The Coachella Valley Mountains Conservancy (the "Conservancy") is a state agency governed by a 20-member Governing Board charged with protecting the natural and cultural

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-086
Page No. 2

resources of the Coachella Valley. Among other things, the Conservancy administrates a grant
program funded by Proposition 1, the "Water Quality, Supply, and Infrastructure Improvement Act
of 2014." The Conservancy's Proposition 1 Grant Program provides funding to public agencies and
nonprofit organizations that respond to drought, conserve water resources, and improve watershed
quality. Conservancy staff reviews applications for Program grants under the supervision of the
Conservancy's Executive Director, the Executive Director presents funding recommendations to the
Conservancy's Governing Board, and the Governing Board awards the grants.

The Palm Springs Cemetery District (the "District") is preparing an application for a
$54,000 grant under the Proposition 1 Grant Program to remove tamarisk trees from an
undeveloped portion of its real property and replace them with native trees or shrubs. Tamarisk
trees are an invasive species that use extraordinary amounts of water and negatively impact native
plant species that provide more suitable habitat for several species of birds. The District's objective
for the tamarisk tree removal project is to reduce the demand on its irrigation wells.

You are the Executive Director of the Conservancy. You own your residence in Cathedral
City, and the property line of your residence is approximately 150 feet from the District's real
property at issue and approximately 661 feet from the nearest trees that the District would remove
pursuant to the grant. Your residence is within a gated community, and the District's real property
is not accessible from within the gated community. Your residence's water is supplied by the local
water district and is not from a well.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An
official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are
identified in Section 87103. With respect to decisions relating to the District's grant application, the
facts presented indicate you would have a real property interest in your residence assuming you
have an interest in that property worth $2,000 or more (see Section 87103(b)), and an interest in
your personal finances and those of immediate family members (see Section 87103).

Foreseeability and Materiality

Regulation 18701(a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or
contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a
decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides
that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Neither your real property interest in your residence nor your interest in your personal
finances is explicitly involved in decisions relating to the District's grant application.

File No. A-19-086
Page No. 3

Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's
reasonably foreseeable effect on an official's real property interest.2 Notwithstanding Regulation
18702.2, however, Regulation 18702(b) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's
interest is not material if it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. Although your residence is
located approximately 150 feet from the District's real property at issue, the nearest tamarisk tree
on that real property that would be removed pursuant to the grant is approximately 661 feet from
your residence. Your residence is within a gated community, the District's real property is not
accessible from within the gated community, and your residence's water is supplied by the local
water district and is not from a well. Therefore, the facts presented indicate that the effect of
decisions relating to the District's grant application on your real property interest in your residence
would be nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant. Accordingly, the Act does not prohibit you
from taking part in those decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

[illegible signature]

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:aja

___________________________
2 Regulation 18702.5 sets forth the Act's materiality standard applicable to a decision's effect on an official's
interest in his or her personal finances. Subdivision (c) of that regulation, however, provides that if the decision at issue
affects the official's real property interest, materiality is determined under Regulation 18702.2.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit you, the Executive Director of the Coachella Valley Mountains 
Conservancy, from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the Palm Springs Cemetery 
District's application for a grant under the Conservancy's Proposition 1 Grant Program to remove 
tamarisk trees from real property owned by the District and replace those trees with native trees or 
shrubs given that your residence is located approximately 150 feet from that real property and 
approximately 661 feet from the nearest trees that the District would remove pursuant to the grant?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in decisions relating to the District's 
grant application because those decisions' effect on your real property interest in your residence 
would be nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant.
